coredata.dc:title: Using silver nanoparticle and thiol graphene quantum dots nanocomposite as a substratum to load antibody for detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen: Electrochemical oxidation of riboflavin was used as redox probe
coredata.dc:creator: Valipour, Akram
Roushani, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: In this study a facile green approach to employ silver nanoparticle (AgNPs) and thiol graphene quantum dots (GQD-SH) as the nanomaterial for ultrasensitive and selective detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen (HCV) have been investigated. AgNPs/GQD-SH was utilized as a substratum to load antibody for detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen. AgNPs have been immobilized on SH groups of GQDs via bonding formation of Ag-S and anti-HCV have been loaded on the electrode surface via the interaction between –NH2 group of antibody and AgNPs. Using the proposed nanocomposite provides a specific platform with increased surface which is capable of loading more antibodies to entrap the antigen. The decreasing of the electrochemical signal can be achieved after the specific recognition between antibodies and antigens. Riboflavin was used as a biological molecule with inherent properties, for the first time, as the redox probe in the development of HCV core antigen electrochemical immunosensor. Compared to the other redox probes, riboflavin is superior in its oxidization in negative potential range, where the number of interfering species for riboflavin is much fewer. The proposed immunosensor showed wide linear range from 0.05pgmL−1 to 60ngmL−1 with limit of detection of 3fgmL−1. This novel immunosensor was used to analyze the serum sample. The immunosensor provides a convenient, low-cost and simple method for HCV core antigen detection and proposes new horizons for quantitative detection of antigen in the clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Riboflavin
GQD-SH
Electrochemical immunosensing
Hepatitis C virus core antigen
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the HS-GQDs and AgNPs/HS-GQDs Nanocomposites
Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise-modified electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Typical EIS studies of [Fe(CN)6]3 −/4− (5mM, KCl 0.1M), in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.0) at (a) GCE (b) GQD-SH/GCE (c) AgNPs/GQD-SH/GCE (d) Ab/AgNPs/GQD-SH/GCE (e) BSA/Ab/AgNPs/GQD-SH/GCE (B) Recorded CVs for the different steps of the modified electrode: (a) to (e) are the same as (A).

(A) DPV responses of the immunosensor with increasing concentrations of HCV core antigen (from up to down: 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 7.5, 15, 30 and 60pgmL−1). (B) Calibration curve of the peak current response versus the logarithm of HCV core antigen concentration. The parameters of DPV are as follows: initial potential, −0.55V; final potential, −0.35V; amplitude, 25mV; modulation time of 50ms.

Amperometric response of the immunosensor to interferes: glucose (A), Progestron (B), Histidin (C), Trypsin (D) and HCV (E). Red lines are attribute interferes and HCV. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the proposed electrochemical immunosensor for HCV core antigen detection.
Detection of HCV core antigen in real samples.

head.ce:title: Using silver nanoparticle and thiol graphene quantum dots nanocomposite as a substratum to load antibody for detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen: Electrochemical oxidation of riboflavin was used as redox probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study a facile green approach to employ silver nanoparticle (AgNPs) and thiol graphene quantum dots (GQD-SH) as the nanomaterial for ultrasensitive and selective detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen (HCV) have been investigated. AgNPs/GQD-SH was utilized as a substratum to load antibody for detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen. AgNPs have been immobilized on SH groups of GQDs via bonding formation of Ag-S and anti-HCV have been loaded on the electrode surface via the interaction between –NH2 group of antibody and AgNPs. Using the proposed nanocomposite provides a specific platform with increased surface which is capable of loading more antibodies to entrap the antigen. The decreasing of the electrochemical signal can be achieved after the specific recognition between antibodies and antigens. Riboflavin was used as a biological molecule with inherent properties, for the first time, as the redox probe in the development of HCV core antigen electrochemical immunosensor.

Compared to the other redox probes, riboflavin is superior in its oxidization in negative potential range, where the number of interfering species for riboflavin is much fewer. The proposed immunosensor showed wide linear range from 0.05pgmL−1 to 60ngmL−1 with limit of detection of 3fgmL−1. This novel immunosensor was used to analyze the serum sample. The immunosensor provides a convenient, low-cost and simple method for HCV core antigen detection and proposes new horizons for quantitative detection of antigen in the clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatitis C is an infection caused by the hepatitis C virus (HCV) that attacks the liver and leads to inflammation. The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates that about 3% of the world's population has been infected by HCV and that there are more than 170 million chronic carriers who are at the risk of developing liver cirrhosis and liver cancer. Thus accurate and sensitive diagnosis of HCV in blood samples during the early stages of infection is very important.

Several studies have revealed accurate and sensitive diagnosis of HCV in blood samples but a few of them (Icardi et al., 2001; Leary et al., 2006; Morota et al., 2009; Tanaka et al., 2000; Valcavi et al., 2004; Ma et al., 2013, 2012; Aoyagi et al., 1999) uses HCV core antigen levels rather than anti-HCV and HCV RNA. In the last decade, HCV core antigen levels have been suggested as the potential markers for viral replication. HCV core antigen has been reported to appear earlier than anti-HCV antibody and may be detected within 1 or 2 days following the appearance of HCV RNA in the serum (Ma et al., 2013).

During recent years, conventional diagnostic methods, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and chemiluminescence have been the main methods used for detection of antigenes. Compared with conventional immunoassays, electrochemical immunoassay has exhibited several advantages including simplicity of instrument, low cost, feasibility of miniaturization and subsequent portability (Roushani et al., 2016).

In the development of electrochemical immunosensing strategies, the signal amplification, sensitivity and stability are key factors in the improving of immunosensors performance. The signal amplification in the electrochemical immunosesnors can be achieved by using nanomaterials such as graphene (Roushani and Valipour, 2016), quantum dots (Zhang et al., 2013) and metal nanoparticles (Roushani et al., 2016). GQDs are graphene sheets with lateral size smaller than 100nm in single, double and multiple layers, and diameters spanning the range 3–20nm mainly (Benítez-Martínez and Valcárcel, 2014). Compared with other counterparts such as semiconductor QDs, GQDs are superior in terms of low cytotoxicity, ease of production, chemical inertness, resistance to photo-bleaching, and excellent biocompatibility (Pei et al., 2015). These properties make them quite promising in sensors and biosensors. Thio-GQDs (SH-GQD) are a class of quantum dots that were found to form stable colloidal suspensions in various solvents including ethanol and dimethylformamide. Nonetheless, the water solubility of the SH-GQD is lower than that of the ox-GQD (Minati et al., 2012). These advantages make it a suitable choice and it seems that utilizing of nanoparticles along with SH-GQD as a specific platform can be a good choice in designing of electrochemical sensors and immunosensor. Noble metal nanoparticles such as gold nanoparticles (Zhang et al., 2016), platinum nanoparticle, and palladium (Li et al., 2011; Wei et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2013) silver nanoparticle (Liu et al., 2014), are the kinds of nanomaterial that have been developed to improve the sensitivity and performance of electrochemical immunosensor. These NPs have several advantages including strong adsorption ability, simplicity of preparation process, cost-effective manufacturing method, good conductivity and large specific surface area. These features make them as an ideal candidate for preparing immunosensors in electrochemical analysis. Silver nanomaterials are more cost effective in their preparation compared to gold and platinium nanomaterials, and they exhibit much higher extinction coefficients than gold nanoparticles of the same size (Wang et al., 2011). Silver nanoparticles as noble metal nanoparticles can strongly interact with thiol group of GQD and can utilize as an intermediator to immobilize antibody in the construction of immunosensor. Furthermore, large amounts of antibodies can be immobilized on the GQD-SH due to its large specific surface area and good biocompatibility. The interaction between thiol group of GQD-SH and silver nanoparticles, resulted in covalent immobilization of silver nanoparticles onto the surface of the electrode. Also an interaction between amino group of antibodies and silver nanoparticles resulted in covalent immobilization of antibodies on the surface of the silver nanoparticles.

Electron mediators should be attached to the electrode surface or dissolved in the sensing solution in order to achieve a sensitive and stable electrochemical response that is a key factor in fabrication of electrochemical sensors and immunosensors. Several redox probe have been introduced in the literature reports such as Rutin (Roushani and Valipour, 2016), potassium hexacyanoferrates (Simon and Fabregas, 2004), ferrocene and its derivatives (Razumiene et al., 2000), catechol (Mazloum-Ardakani and Hosseinzadeh, 2016), hydroquinone (Li et al., 2011) and riboflavin (6,7-dimethyl-9-(d-1-ribityl)-isoalloxazine) (Roushani and Abdi, 2014). Compared to these redox probes, riboflavin is superior in its oxidization in negative potential range, where the number of interfering species for riboflavin is much fewer, while other probes are oxidized in the positive potential range where many species are presence such as dopamine oxidized.

Herein, we developed a facile green approach to employ AgNPs and GQD-SH as the nanomaterial for ultrasensitive and selective detection of HCV core antigen. In the proposed method, the NH2 group of antibody was covalent attachment to the AgNPs attached to the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) which is modified by GQD-SH. To evaluate the utility of the riboflavin as a probe for immunosensing application, it was used for the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) detection of HCV core antigen. To the best of our knowledge, an application of BSA/Ab/AgNPs/GQD-SH/GCE and riboflavin, a probe for the detection of HCV, has never been reported yet.

All chemicals used in the study were of analytical reagent grade purity. Antigen and antibody, dimethylformamide (DMF), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), riboflavin, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, AgNO3 and all other reagents ordered from commercial suppliers (Sigma-Aldrich, Merck or Fluka Company).

Spectroscopic characterizations of GQD-SH were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer) and UV–Vis spectroscopy (VARIAN 300 Bio CARY). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations were carried out using an electron microscope (Hitachi H-800). The fluorescence spectra were obtained via a Carry Eclipse (Agilent, Fluorescence and Phosphorescence spectrometer). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) (model nanosurf Mobile S software version 1.8) at operating mode of dynamic force and non-contact scan type was used. All electrochemical experimental data including cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out with a μ-AUTOLAB electrochemical system type III and FRA2 board computer controlled Potentiostate/Galvanostate (Eco-Chemei, Switzerland) driven with NOVA software. A three electrode system including a modified GCE as a working electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode and a platinum wire as a counter electrode was used. The DPV measurements were carried out by scanning the potential of −0.55 to −0.35V with modulation time of 50ms and modulation amplitude of 25 mv. EIS analysis was carried out with a bias potential of 0.2V and a frequency range between 0.1Hz and 100kHz with signal amplitude of 5mV. A Metrohm model 780pH/mV meters was used to measure the pH.

GQD-SH was synthesized according to a previous report by some modification (Minati et al., 2012). Briefly, 0.1g MWCNTs powder was added to 10mL of HNO3/H2SO4 mixture at a ratio of 1:3 and was sonicated for 6h at 40°C to yield oxidized MWCNTs (ox-MWCNTs). The obtained black suspension containing ox-MWCNTs was diluted with distillated water and allowed to stand overnight for precipitation. Then the supernatant was removed and the suspension of ox-CNT was diluted with deionized water and filtered with a 0.05mm filter membrane under vacuum. In order to form a stable colloidal suspensions the ox-CNT were dissolved in a plenty of solvents including water, ethanol and dimethylformamide. 1mL of 1mmolL−1 cysteamine solution in ethanol was added to the resulted suspension, mixed (24h) and then decanted overnight. Cysteamine leads to isolating of NPs which is named as oxidized GQD (ox-GQD) on the CNTs suspension. 10mmolL−1 of DCC solution in DMF was added to the collected ox-GQD to start the coupling reaction and it was mixed for 24. Finally, the GQD were purified by repeated filtration through a cut-off membrane (MW 1500) and rinsed with fresh DMF. In this way GQD-SH was achieved.

The size of antibody or antigen is about 10–15nm which is much smaller than that of 100nm (traditional size of nanomaterial), so nanomaterials with large scale may not obtain a satisfied result due to an inadequate adsorption. Thus the size of synthesized nanoparticle should be small. Thus based on a previously described method that considers this point, AgNPs was synthesized (Wang et al., 2011). In short, 20mL of 1.0mmolL−1 AgNO3 aqueous solution, 0.5mL of 0.1molL−1 sodium citrate solutions and 1mL of 3.5mmolL−1 NaBH4 was mixed together with considering some limitations such as making freshly prepared aqueous solution containing sodium borohydride and keeping it on ice to reduce the rate of decomposition during the experiment. The prepared solution was added dropwise to 200mL boiled water which had been cooled. Then, it was stirred vigorously for 2h and a transparent yellow homogenous colloidal solution of silver nanoparticles was obtained. The synthesized silver nanoparticles (13nm) were characterized by TEM that shows the successful synthesis of silver nanoparticles.

Before fabrication, the GCE was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05µm alumina powder and then thoroughly cleaned with deionized water. The cleaned electrode was covered by 10μL of GQD-SH solution and dried in the room temperature. Subsequently, solution of AgNPs was dropped onto the surface of the modified GCE. After 3h, AgNPs was attached on thiol groups of the GQD-SH/GCE surface. Then 10μL of the NH2-Ab solution was incubated onto the surface of the obtained AgNPs/ GQD-SH/GCE in order to immobilize anti-HCV molecules on the surface of silver nanoparticles by chemisorption between silver nanoparticles and amino groups of anti-HCV core antigen. Then 10μL of BSA (10%) solution was used to cover the surface of electrode to reduce the effective surface area and available active sites and avoid non-specific adsorption ( Scheme 1).

Several techniques were applied to investigate the successfully synthesize of the GQD-SH. Structural characterizations of GQD-SH was confirmed using TEM and also spectroscopic characterizations of it were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Fluorescence and UV–VIS spectroscopy. The morphology of the structure of the GQD-SH, was recorded by TEM. Successful synthesis of GQD-SH also could be confirmed by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectra of GQD-SH, are given in Fig. S1A, the adsorption peaks at 2872 and 2933cm−1 corresponds to aliphatic C-H band. The absorbance band at 3520cm−1 is corresponded to hydroxyl groups in the GQD-SH and also the existing of an absorbance band at 2507cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of GQD-SH manifested successful functionalization of the GQD with thiol group which is consistent with the literature result. As shown in the Fig. S1B, it demonstrates that the GQD-SH particle size is about 100nm in diameter, which could be served as a good path way for increasing the surface area of the modified electrode. From the UV–vis absorption in DMF (Fig. S1C), an absorption band at around 370nm was observed as likewise previous reports (Minati et al., 2012). Fluorescent measurement was recorded (Fig. S1D) and an intense emission which is in agreement with other results reported in literature for fluorescent GQDs (Minati et al., 2012). All these techniques prove the successfully synthesis of GQD-SH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the HS-GQDs and AgNPs/HS-GQDs Nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Di-dimensional and three-dimensional AFM images of GQDs (Fig. S2A and B) and AgNPs/ SH-GQDs (C and D) were investigated. From Fig. S2, it could be found that the GQDs-SH have been immobilized and well-dispersed on the electrode. When the AgNPs conjugated with SH groups on the GQDs-SH/GCE film, as Fig. S2C shows, the GQDs-SH are covered with AgNPs homogeneously around the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise-modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: EIS was used for characterization of the surface features of the modified electrodes at each immobilization step. Fig. 1A shows the Nyquist plots of impedance spectroscopy of bare electrode and different modified electrodes in 5mmolmL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution. Based on this technique the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is a key parameter that shows the immobilization of the substrates onto the electrode surface in each step of the process. After casting some of GQD-SH on the surface of GCE, Rct value increased from 1028±10Ω for the bare GCE (curve a) to 1410±3.1Ω for the GQD-SH/GCE (curve b). The existence of the thiol and hydroxyl groups in the structure of the GQD-SH leads to negative partial charge of this nanomaterial. So, the presence of the negative partial charge on the GCE surface leads to [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− repulsion and electron transfer decreasing. In the next step, Rct value increased (curve c, Rct=1805±5Ω) when silver nanoparticles was dropped on the surface of the electrode. The presence of AgNPs with negative charge, immobilizing onto the SH groups of GQDs via bonding formation of Ag-S, repelled the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3 −/4− anions to the extent that the response of redox probe was reduced and thereby led to electron-transfer resistance enhancement. In the next step, Rct was increased to 5129±5.4Ω by loading anti-HCV on the electrode surface via the interaction between –NH2 group of antibody and AgNPs. The similar increasing also could be observed when BSA solution was dropped onto the surface of electrode (curve e, Rct=6116±3.5Ω). The increased resistance in curve “d” and curve “e” can be attributed to the reducing of available active sites and the effective surface area for electron transfer process by Ab and BSA. As a result, the immunosensor has been constructed successfully.

Also CV is a favored technique to study the feature of the surface-modified electrodes. Fig. 1B demonstrates the CV of different modification steps that were carried out in 5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 − which includes KCl (0.1molL−1). First, a CV of the bare electrode (GCE) was recorded (curve a). Second, the peak current was decreased when the GQD-SH was dropped onto the surface of electrode which is attributed to repelling between negative partial charge of GQD-SH and the probe. This phenomena lead to inhibiting electron transfer between redox probe and the electrode surface (curve b). The peak current further decreased when the AgNPs were added onto the surface of electrode because of the negative charge. Third, casting the Ab molecules on the electrode surface resulted in more blocking of electron flow of [Fe(CN)6]3 −/4− to the electrode surface (curve d). Finally, the amperometric response decreased with the dropping of BSA solution onto electrode surface. This is attributed to reducing available active sites for electron transfer process by protein. The results were the same as that of present in EIS.

The pH value of solution and the incubation time are of vital importance for the analytical performance of immunosensors. Thus, we checked the importance of these factors. The effect of phosphate buffer solution pH on the peak current was studied in the pH range of 5.5–8.0 (Fig. S3A). It was found that the peak current increased when the pH value increased from 5.5 to 7.0. When the pH value was above 7.0, the response decreased with the increasing of pH value. Therefore, a pH 7.0 of the working buffer was applied for further experiments. The pH of the solution has great effects on the electrochemical performance of the immunosensor so that in strong acidic and alkaline solutions the amperometric signal decreases. For the detection of the sufficient recognition time of target protein, the incubation time was investigated. It was found that the peak current intensity increased as the incubation time increased from 10 to 30min, and it reached a maximum value at 30min. Thus 30min was chosen as the optimal incubation time (Fig. S3B).

Under optimal conditions, the DPV response of the electrochemical immunosensor to HCV core antigen was performed in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.5mmolL−1 riboflavin. Fig. 2 shows the DPV responses of the probe related to the addition of the different concentration of HCV core antigen. It was notable that peak currents decreased with the increasing of the concentration of HCV core antigen, which was due to the formation of increasing the number of antigen/antibody complex, with the result of the enhanced hindering of electron transfer reaction of riboflavin on the sensing surface. The regression equation between the changes of electrochemical current response and the logarithm value of different concentrations of HCV core antigen ranging from 0.05 to 60pgmL−1 was found; ΔI(µA)=0.1838 lodsaagC+0.3788 (As shown in Fig. 2B the changes of reduction peak current increased with increasing concentration of HCV-core antigen). The correlation coefficient was 0.9924 and the limit of detection was calculated as 3.0fgmL−1 (S/N=3). Each point of the calibration graph corresponds to the mean value obtained from 3 independent measurements, and the error bars show the standard deviations of the measurements. The formula used for LOD is LOD=3S/0.1838 (S: the standard deviation of the 20 times of the background signal).

So far a few of electrochemical immunosensor have been constructed to detect HCV core antigen that are listed in Table S1. The performance of the developed immunosensor was superior to that of the other electrochemical immunosensors reported in the related literature, especially in comparison to the reported LODs. Furthermore, the simple fabrication process and the use of cost-effective and biocompatible compounds are major advantages in comparison to different HCV core antigen immunosensors. The sensitive DPV detection performance was attributed to (1) High surface to volume ratio and electronic structure of GQD-SH (2) Covalent attachment of AgNPs with thiol groups of GQD-SH. 3) Large surface area of AgNPs that causes the large amounts of anti-HCV immobilized on the electrode surface. 4) Covalent attachment of AgNPs with amino groups of anti-HCV leads to more stability and repeatability in comparison to the adsorption method.

Intra-electrode and inter-electrode coefficients of variation were used in order to investigate the reproducibility. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of reproducibility was 5.3% for 5 times measuring HCV core antigen with the different immunosensors (Inter-electrode). Also for five times the reproducibility of the immunosensor was estimated by determining HCV with one immunosensor (Intra-electrode) and RSD was calculated 3.1%.

In the practical application the stability of the immunosensor is also a key factor. Thus, the immunosensor was stored at refrigerated conditions (4°C) and used periodically for the analysis of HCV core antigen at an interval of 5 days. After 20 days, no apparent change was found toward HCV core antigen with similar concentration and more than 90% of the initial response was retained.

To evaluate the selectivity of the HCV core antigen immunosensor in human serum sample, the DPV responses were recorded for some molecules such as glucose (Glu), Progestron (Prog), Histidin (Hist), and Trypsin (Tryp). When 0.5pgmL−1 HCV core antigen and 500 times of interfering substances were incubated on to the immunosensor separately, no significant changes was found for interfering substances compared with the initial response of the immunosensor but a well-defined change was observed in the response for HCV core antigen. The current response variation due to the interference was less than 5% of that without the interference, indicating the selectivity of the proposed immunosensor was acceptable. Fig. 3 suggests a good specificity of the fabricated immunosensor.

The immunosensor was tested in order to confirm the accuracy and precision. The immunosensor was used to detect the recoveries of different concentrations of HCV core antigen in human serum samples. Human serum samples were ordered from a local clinical laboratory and subjected to ultrafiltration by loading into a centrifugal filtration tube at 3000rpm (30min). Afterwards, the serum samples were diluted 50 times with PBS (0.1molL−1) and different concentrations of HCV core antigen were spiked to these samples. As it can be seen, the recovery was in the range from 95% to 110% and the RSD was in the range from 0.1% to 0.3%, indicating that the precision of this method is good (Data are shown in Table 1).

A new label-free electrochemical immunosensor was prepared, which avoids labeling either antibodies or antigens, to make the detection of HCV core antigen cheaper, faster and simpler. The developed method provides a promising Ag/GQD-SH nanocomposite and riboflavin as a universal redox probe for different analytes. GQD-SH was successfully synthesized and used to modify the working electrode. AgNPs have been immobilized onto the SH groups of GQDs via bonding formation of Ag-S and anti-HCV have been loaded on the electrode surface via the interaction between –NH2 group of antibody and AgNPs. After modification, the working electrode possessed high bioactivity and could be used to detect HCV core antigen. Also the work will focus on the application of this immunosensor to other antigen. The immunosensor showed high efficiency and high sensitivity, and it was demonstrated to be an effective tool for the research of HCV core antigen detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Ilam for financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.09.086.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
